result:
LAST LIGHT OVER THE TIDEPOOLS

I start with the eye of an octopus I saw yesterday evening, because the colour that welled up in its iris was the cadmium yellow of used bookstore spines and because, when the animal shot a single arm across my boot to test whether I was rock or food or neither, the limb was the exact grey-beige of the coffee-soaked notebooks under my bed. My mind keeps returning to that colour as though it is a toggle between this world and an older one where chroma was memory rather than mere optics; I have resolved to let the toggle stay half-thrown.

        I had walked to the tidepools to retrieve an aluminium spoon once meant for my grandmother’s silverware but stolen decades ago by my father and later by me, bent into a narrow arc and tied to my key-ring. I pried oysters with it in those years when I lived aboard a junk-rigged sloop on the Inside Passage, and at some point its bowl had become coral-etched, each scratch a date of someone’s birthday I was missing while I scratched barnacles. In the dream I had last week I used the same spoon to taste moonlight over the water; it tasted not of silver but of raw nickel, metallic enough to burn the tongue yet oddly sweet. When I woke I decided the spoon, which I had not seen in months, must be dredged back to me. The octopus merely happened to be wearing the right colour to greet it.

        Such coincidences keep threading the days lately. Two weeks earlier I had sold the typewriter which for twelve years typed the addresses on envelopes for a monthly newsletter I never mailed. Then, on the seventh night after the sale, I walked past a pawnshop lit by a single fluorescent tube and saw the same machine standing among violins and rusted trumpet mutes, price tag dangling like a forlorn ticket stub. I have sometimes held the prejudice that the cosmos prefers stopping the clocks just before last call, when every object shines with a sudden significance that will evaporate with daylight. Seeing the typewriter again felt like the bartender pouring a final round against his own rules; I did not go in, but I felt the warmth of that rule’s waiver.

        Yesterday, pausing between boulders at the furthest reach of the tide’s recession, I tried to understand why certain objects—this spoon, that’s typewriter, the contradistinctive note of perfume a stranger left on the tattered scarf of a park-bench philosopher near the wharf—burn with private importance while the planet itself seems allergic to ceremony. I thought of a phrase I once read on a burner account in a defunct online forum: the universe is a palimpsest revealed only when you misremember a line from a song. I rolled it around until it sounded like prayer. Then the octopus breathed out that yellow and reached its little grey arm.

        The arm did not want the spoon; the arm wanted to know whether the hand that held the spoon could be tasted without consequence. It tasted the stainless steel and the wool cuff and then retreated, clearly disappointed. I felt, without vanity, that the disappointment implied I still possessed insufficient seasoning: too much inland melancholy, not enough iodine. The water had already lifted to my ankles. When I lifted the spoon, moon-flecked water slid off it like mercury off glass, and in the puddle where it had been I recognized the outline of a continent—jagged coast, interior panhandle, island chain—exactly matching the island my brother drew once on a napkin in a diner after he had enlisted and before he vanished over an actual horizon. I crouched longer than was safe, knees rimed by the advancing tide, trying to decide whether resemblance implies invitation, until the shape dissolved and the trough needed refilling.

        On the way back to the esplanade the streetlights shuddered to life and night threw its first veil over the warehouses. I had turned on my phone’s flashlight—or rather, the phone had turned itself on, for reasons modernity pretends are safeguards but are more likely loneliness—and the blue-white screen summoned moths that had not yet evolved colour receptors for that particular hue. One of the larger moths wore a scalloped wing-edge shaped uncannily like the fragment of maple leaf my mother once glued to the inside of a jar lid when we made firefly lanterns. The nostalgia rose so fast I tasted iron in my throat before I identified the source: my tongue had found a tiny fleck of grit the spoon had dislodged from an oyster shell. The iron reminded me of the blood in the water the last time I tried to explain déjà vu to another person. I let the moth spiral away and told the phone to shut up.

        At the hostel dormitory I share with three seasonal workers, two human backpacks, a ukulele missing a string, and—somehow—a half-grown ficus whose leaves keep turning different shades depending on which bunk’s dream is uppermost, I set the spoon on the sill above my mattress and narrated silently: Here lies the instrument of itinerant oysters and inherited ants and lunar broth: use only at low tide. One bunk over, Leila—who claims to have been born aboard a freight train between Montreal and a declared nowhere—asked in voice husky with half-sleep whether spoons could remember caviar. Before answer could clot, the upstairs neighbour began to conduct an argument with a recording of themselves producing the argument, audio leaking through pine boards like time leaking from a poorly stoppered hourglass. The argument seemed to be about whether future regrets can pre-layout their own carpets; someone kept repeating “we die because wallpaper can’t spell.” None of us called the landlord. Instead Leila’s ficus unfurled a single new leaf the colour of circus peanut candy. I closed eyes against design and listened to the city cough through distant cranes.

        By morning the spoon was gone—taken by gravity, kleptomaniac wind, or the sleepwalking man in the opposite bunk who collects smooth stones. I did not waste breath reproaching randomness. I walked instead to the café shaped like an inkwell, ordered coffee in a cracked porcelain cup whose handle felt familiar the way déjà vu once felt before the etymologists neutered it. While I stirred sugar, the barista began swapping ornaments (corks, glass beads, a single mitten) among the necks of empty wine bottles above the register. With each switch I felt a cord tug beneath my ribcage until the last bottle displayed a cork whose underside carried the inked initial my father used on the faded baggage tag still stapled in the back of my childhood atlas. The cord registered neither welcome nor warning; merely tension.

        A woman at the corner table was writing postcards using a pen filled with what looked, at three strides’ distance, like squid ink. She crossed out lines visible only when the angle of the overhead bulb flashed ultraviolet, then sealed each card with wax the colour of bruised plums. I counted seven cards completed before her luggage of gesture folded into stillness. One postcard she addressed in miniature calligraphy: The Sound Between Islands. She wrote the return address as the latitude of the foghorn I once mistook for my own pulse on a ferry crossing at seventeen. I did not approach; sometimes watching from the rind is closer to the pulp than any bite.

        Outside, a boy wheeling a cello in a red wagon shouted coordinates over his shoulder at a woman walking pigeons on leashes made of shoelaces. The woman translated the numbers into a rhythmic coo. After three iterations the birds lifted as one, taking the recollection of latitude away into blank sky. None of the onlookers clapped. It was that hour when magic survives thanks merely to lack of rehearsal.

        I mention these events sequentially but I feel them more like sheets thrown in the same gust: separate laundry, entire wind. Memory used to have a spine, a narrow one beside the atlas’s gutter. I would flip to it the way other boys flipped to mecca points marked in paper atlases. Now it comes loose, parchment confetti ready to be re-inhaled.  

        Once, a librarian told me that we catalog according to shock waves rather than topic. She prescribed me the shelf harbouring 551.21—submarine canyons—but slipped between the folios a photograph of people lined up for bread in a city just bombed flat. “The canyon below wave base and the queue at eye level,” she said, “share curvature of longing.” I thought that was melodrama until I found the same curvature in the hipbone of someone I loved on their side in moonlight, the slight hollow carrying shadow like a canyon whose echo forgets its own name yet continues sounding. She left when the next war elsewhere preempted the horizon for both of us.

        Trying not to chase her footsteps on that same trajectory, I ride buses that announce stops in languages no rider understands. I treat the city as if it were winter-gutted piano—soundboard exposed to weather, hammers banging up solitude between ribs of streets. The artist on billboards this season admonishes us in four-storey font: DO NOT TURN THIS SACRED SIGN INTO DECOR. The piece is itself a billboard whose vinyl skin peels like birch bark. I recognise the kerning from a church bulletin printed the year the day my mother slipped communion bread into her coat pocket, wanting to keep a miracle from being digested. Sacred excess stales quickly into décor, she told us later, but a retained host becomes a yeast capable of growing resentment or devotion alike. She let mould bloom inside that pocket rather than choose.

        Several nights ago, on a concrete terrace overlooking a river laced with oil slick, I met a man cutting lengths of fishing line for someone who had already gone to jail for snapping, as he put it, “the umbilical to the overground.” The line he measured glinted like strewn vertebrae. While he spoke, dragonflies kept landing on his forearm as if to reassemble the missing words between his sentences. He handed me a piece: eight inches, precise. Said if ever I needed to anchor myself against rising floodwater, tie it tight. I laughed, thanked him, pocketed the tether. Only later did I note the knot already tied at one end bore identical geometry to the slip knot I used on the last letter my brother mailed before vanishing. The envelope had contained only that knot, paper blank. I hadn’t understood until now why. The letter was never meant to be read; it was meant to be ballast.

        I place the eight-inch line now under my pillow next to a lens from an old slide projector. The lens focuses no image; tonight its purpose is weight. In the bunk above, someone turns restlessly so that a single strand of hair falls, static-clung, onto my cheek like tentative antenna. I breathe her probably-color-insomnia plume of lemon and vanillin and coffee grounds. The window is cracked open; the ficus struggles its potted sea legs toward a diminished moon. I whisper to the dark, intentionally misquoting Neruda: “I want to do to you what the spring does with the spoon.” A muffled laugh echoes from some other dread, but downstairs and across the alley a different laugh returns, higher, older, both tones braided by night until I cannot tell who responds to whom across decades and cornices. Laughter superimposes itself like double exposures of film. We are all being sung through.

        Dawn finds me standing at the harbor rail where gulls drop shells onto asphalt hoping gravity will finish what their beaks won’t. The tide has not yet returned the spoon; perhaps it has become the hinge in an oyster's hinge, or the letter O in a child's attempt at a colander compass. Let it remain lowercase and subsumed. I have other implements. Across the water rises the hulk of an unfinished bridge—arches begun, then abandoned when budgets orphaned the beams. Today, heavy mist cloaks the gaps so that each arch floats unattached—brutalist cumulus. Boats whisper underneath like rumours across a theatre lobby between acts. From this vantage I can almost see the island in the napkin outline from the diner, but only if I squint past the mist. Doing so makes my eyes water; the watering reminds me I haven’t cried since watching a woman on subway steps play accordion while balancing a laughing baby on her stomach instrument. The accordion’s song had been achingly familiar yet unplaceable, a tune I must have once sung but in a dream whose setting had been erased. Maybe tears are the only music older than chords.

        Today is the anniversary of my brother’s departure, or what passes for anniversary when disappearance is continuous. The news agencies issued no report; islands do not vanish, people do. Yet I keep the day anyway as a private alternative calendar dip in the river without leaving footprints. I will burn no photograph, intone no name, but I will carry out the ritual we invented as boys: collect one rock for every direction visible from a highway overpass, pocket them for seven days, then cast them back over the rail hoping to strike the windshields of drivers who need a small tremor to re-ask the small essential anything. I lack the heart for highway overpass acoustics now, so I adapt. I choose a driftwood plank, write the same seven cardinal verbs we once whispered—vanish, forgive, swallow, echo, listen, hush, stay—in fading sharpie along its grain. At twilight I will set it on the sea’s edge and let tide tongue erase whichever word the water thinks deserves forgetting.

        Between here and then I sit among fish crates stacking phone books older than myself. Someone discarded a whole run from 1993-2009. The spines smell of pulped cologne and discounted rain. I pull volumes at random, tear strips like palm fronds to make impromptu cranes—or planes—or prayers—and toss them into the wind that sweeps between warehouses. Their flight paths vary: some skid like ash along the ink-rift cement, some pull upward with surprising bravery into hail of gulls. One catches an updraft, becomes yellowed everywhere in momentary prism of hive light before disintegrating against matte sky. I think each fragment returns a fraction of the human density once compressed between columns: GARAGE SALE—SPACE NEEDED. JUNIOR NEEDS UNIFORM. STRAY CAT pregnant. The metaphysics of small desires made newsprint, preserved until acquittal by wind. Disposed into shift.

        Back at the hostel Leila has located the missing spoon; it sits now upright inside a preserved jam jar full of cloudy water, along with a thimbleful of plumeria seeds and a folded scrap bearing a single word written in an alphabet I cannot parse. She claims the spoon and jar cured her insomnia, which had bothered her since waking inside the wrong year. I ask the story but she only smiles, initialed spoon spinning slowly within the swirl—tiny propeller—but jam jar globe will not turn the same tide twice. She gestures: tonight the seeds glow faintly, bioluminescent or simply reflecting hostel hallway LED. I settle onto my bunk beneath the slow sparkle, feeling the weight of guitar nobody plays yet nobody removes. Its silence is barricade against applause no audience remembers planning. Outside, an ambulance refrains long enough to let the ficus leaf rattle—a single elegy for continuity.

        Midafternoon, thunderhead mirroring silted river. I carry wood plank to shoreline already salt-white sandpapered. I meant to wait till dusk but the sky has pressed lower and begun flashing parentheses, as if documenting its own condition live. The water today is the colour of graphite rainfall diluted with milk, folding over shards of noonlight like reluctant origami. I kneel, gouge plank slightly into weight’s faith in gravity, set the word-list facing incoming dark. A herring gull lands ten feet away, one eye on me, other on horizon like bifocal moral. Decides I am neither predator nor breadcrumb pilgrimage sponsor, lifts off before verdict currency can be tendered.

        Here is where memory leans most precariously forward: I hear my brother’s voice preceding the thunder by exactly half-second syllable count as though they rehearsed. He always timed his jokes to thunder for maximum drama. The voice says: You still believe distances are measured only painfully? Watch plank. As soon as the plank says it, the first wave—small but certain—touches plank’s furthest edge and begins eroding all verbs simultaneously, letters curling like cellophane set briefly on hearth.

        For a blink every word fights for presences: vanish refuses vanishing, swear becomes fountain pen attempting backward walk; stay curls into anchor chain tying board itself into knot. Yet tidal eraser is stubborn. Within seven swash cycles every verb is gone. Plank blank as before speech. The herring gull has returned, stands on newly-clean slate. Voice again, fading now: distances measured otherwise ache even longer. Thunder rolls in isometric harmony.

        I remain kneeling while rain starts. Umbrella forgotten, I taste salt rain and think of plumeria seeds glowing in jar. Bright inside glass against forgetting. I lift plank—now almost weightless from layer lost—tuck keystone undercoat despite sogginess. Something must outlast moment. May vowel-less plank remember absence better than my notebooks ever managed.

        On return path streetlights still dead from power grid tantrum. Every storefront has pasted illuminated apology: OUR OUTLET TEMPORARILY BENT BY WEATHER. WE WILL REOPEN WHEN SOCKETS ALIGN. Power outages transform entire blocks into cello cases waiting instruments. I move through murk by counting windows reflecting alternate timelines: each lit room shows scenes sketched in colours none of the room’s occupants may ever guess they owned. One window reveals two men arranging flowers upright in river currents—magnetic vases intersect water flow without drifting. Another window displays woman slowly dismantling violin bridge with toothpick, midnight docent for scatter patience. Third window contains simply yellow umbrella opened upside down under exposed ceiling bulb as though practicing emergency catch for indoor lightning. None aware passerby patterns outside. I catalogue without comment, already stuffed full of mismatched atlas nerve endings.

        Hostel lobby re-stitched by generator growl. Lights intermittent like code sent by weary moths. Leila sits on threshold stoop strumming missing-string ukulele numbers. When she sees plank, she says touch word-vacant wood and nods. The absence speaks dialect we newly recognise. Ukulele fourth string replaced suddenly with fishing line retrieved from sea-smell pocket. She holds it against sounds of rain on corrugated tin roof then suddenly knots line through lowest tuning peg, miracle enough to birth entirely fresh chord neither she nor instrument knew possible. I feel keening harmonic against my teeth metal spoon once rested in jaw hollow. Somewhere upstairs generator coughs final dieseline belch, surrendering lobby back partly to candle and partly to dark.

        Night three subsequent: same summer season’s dog end foraging through alleyway bones of dumpsters. Leila and I walk toward river where unfinished bridge now carries temporary torches—welding team repairing epoxied day-after. Workers’ sparks arch mid-air like fireflies manufacturing linear constellations. Leila carries jam jar. Plumeria seeds have sprouted miniature fluorescences during interrupted power, now dance inside glass like plankton lunar repro. We choose spot beneath undecided bridge span: here river’s breadth quiet enough to hear tide reversing under bridge ribcage.

        She tips jar gently onto plank. Seeds scatter, green dots amid tide tangle. Between黑暗 river surface and torch spark above, seeds glow dual sourced: internal sugar and borrowed welding flares. Cube of reflected danger. Spoon settles rattling amidst wheatgrass larval. Leila begins humming ukulele chord re-anchored, but string whistles slightly off-tone now boat wakes lap pylons. She angles spoon so handle becomes stylus against wood bereft-of-letters. Together we wait: what new inscription does  shaken world want burnt upon this breakfast utensil?

        Spoon dances circumlit by torch flare only long enough for two lines to appear. I read them aloud once, then memory rescinds them. Cannot recall glyphs, only feeling they raised like charge through cracked powerline. After sound’s cessation, plank suddenly drenched brighter hollow where words ought to be. Plumeria seedlings root themselves where letters once resisted erosion. It is done. Bridge ahead completes its welding midnight vow, spanning dreamed gorge. Leila stops music mid-chord as cranes switch off, abrupt hush so deep it rust rings in teeth same frequency as spoon’s aftertaste.

        We leave spoon standing upright between planks like lighthouse exorcised of range. I notice on walkback constellation near eastern edge of sky has shifted to resemble outline of childhood island my brother drew: molasses shape, archipelagic tail. Either astronomy has finally conspired or this hemisphere never noticed before. I make no wishes merely observation. When I blink constellation doubles—one higher fresher sibling gently orbiting older version. Between them hangs tiny point-bright asteroid or perhaps satellite—my brother still traveling by vocabulary of distances measured otherwise.

        City returns puddle-grimed consciousness full of second-hand power. Streetlights sputter open eyed. In hostel lobby someone has stuck dried seaweed spelling the word FIN. Letters already curling at tips under fluorescent acne. Fresh mural painted on opposing wall: portrait of last week’s octopus painted entirely in bird-pecked pomegranate rind, one eye visible as daemon amber. Underneath text silhouetted: I TESTED YOU AND BECAME HUNGER. Leila plucks first whole ukulele chord since restringing: clear tonic, evenly ringing like beginning hymn.

        I discover someone has left new parcel on bunk: small matchbox wrapped in map evident to bloodroot paper, no greeting yet signature is faint smell of tidal zinc. Inside box lie seven polished pebbles—exact count of rock ritual my brother and I never finished. Pebbles cool against palm. I pocket them in place where spoons or destinations used to lodge. One pebble small enough to roll inside cheek without impediment, becoming instant worry bead / shutter dispersal when tongue worries meanings slack.

        Later—dawn still unclaimed—I rise to use communal bathroom’s one mirror undistorted by steam. Paint peels back like sour grapeskin, revealing behind plaster an photograph pasted in early hostel years: snapshot of this very bathroom rim crowded with toothbrushes clipped to hanging clothesline above tub. Every brush suspended mid-sob. A date scribbled 1997; who would have predicted its fossil inside this timeline? I peer closer noticing one toothbrush distinctive handle—recognisable as type owned by my grandmother before she gave soothsayer spoons away to impetuous sons. Ghost spatial knot completed.

        At breakfast table Leila spreads spilled sugar into continent shapes. She moves grains with skewer until archipelago forms again the island of diner napkin. I pour coffee into teal cup already holding 1.25oz invisible ocean from last night wave collision. We do not speak, because the world has ribboned temporarily to single thread of saliva-warm pebble coasting inner cheek. When Leila finally pushes sugar island gently towards my mug edge, a cafe sugar storm pelts it into reef without protests of bravery form. Storm subside into depths newly stirred. She raises eyes across table meeting mine as though catching both our reflections inside same convex tablespoon mum used to scale cocoa.

        Somewhere thunder may still rehearse lines above unfinished bridge but river below already carries plank now cleanly letter-less, now home to plumeria root. I know the spoon remains joined upriver, spoon and spoon-shaped governance. Tomorrow hostelry will empty and refill again as tides do lodgers. We who remain within rooms like shells become language when enough listened to break into voice. Outer shell memory remains the same quiet octopus yellow reserve, ileum cadmium, watery grey edging finger. If we are lucky we may taste moonlight with the nickel aftertaste of almost-loss and continue choosing thetoggle forward.

        I finish breakfast leaving pocket stone inside cheek. Chewing time gently: limestone converse. Outside, gulls practice backstroke between streetcar cables. Rind moon carves asphalt puddles ever recalibrate island boundaries drawn by our blinking. I look—not for the last time—towards horizon, and the horizon looks back with one amber eye choosing not to blink first.

In the bunk above, someone turns restlessly so that a single strand of hair falls, static-clung, onto my cheek like tentative antenna. I breathe her probably-color-insomnia plume of lemon and vanillin and coffee grounds. The window is cracked open; the ficus struggles its potted sea legs toward a diminished moon. I whisper to the dark, intentionally misquoting Neruda: “I want to do to you what the spring does with the spoon.” A muffled laugh echoes from some other dread, but downstairs and across the alley a different laugh returns, higher, older, both tones braided by night until I cannot tell who responds to whom across decades and cornices. Laughter superimposes itself like double exposures of film. We are all being sung through.

        Dawn finds me standing at the harbor rail where gulls drop shells onto asphalt hoping gravity will finish what their beaks won’t. The tide has not yet returned the spoon; perhaps it has become the hinge in an oyster's hinge, or the letter O in a child's attempt at a colander compass. Let it remain lowercase and subsumed. I have other implements. Across the water rises the hulk of an unfinished bridge—arches begun, then abandoned when budgets orphaned the beams. Today, heavy mist cloaks the gaps so that each arch floats unattached—brutalist cumulus. Boats whisper underneath like rumours across a theatre lobby between acts. From this vantage I can almost see the island in the napkin outline from the diner, but only if I squint past the mist. Doing so makes my eyes water; the watering reminds me I haven’t cried since watching a woman on subway steps play accordion while balancing a laughing baby on her stomach instrument. The accordion’s song had been achingly familiar yet unplaceable, a tune I must have once sung but in a dream whose setting had been erased. Maybe tears are the only music older than chords.

        Today is the anniversary of my brother’s departure, or what passes for anniversary when disappearance is continuous. The news agencies issued no report; islands do not vanish, people do. Yet I keep the day anyway as a private alternative calendar dip in the river without leaving footprints. I will burn no photograph, intone no name, but I will carry out the ritual we invented as boys: collect one rock for every direction visible from a highway overpass, pocket them for seven days, then cast them back over the rail hoping to strike the windshields of drivers who need a small tremor to re-ask the small essential anything. I lack the heart for highway overpass acoustics now, so I adapt. I choose a driftwood plank, write the same seven cardinal verbs we once whispered—vanish, forgive, swallow, echo, listen, hush, stay—in fading sharpie along its grain. At twilight I will set it on the sea’s edge and let tide tongue erase whichever word the water thinks deserves forgetting.

        Between here and then I sit among fish crates stacking phone books older than myself. Someone discarded a whole run from 1993-2009. The spines smell of pulped cologne and discounted rain. I pull volumes at random, tear strips like palm fronds to make impromptu cranes—or planes—or prayers—and toss them into the wind that sweeps between warehouses. Their flight paths vary: some skid like ash along the ink-rift cement, some pull upward with surprising bravery into hail of gulls. One catches an updraft, becomes yellowed everywhere in momentary prism of hive light before disintegrating against matte sky. I think each fragment returns a fraction of the human density once compressed between columns: GARAGE SALE—SPACE NEEDED. JUNIOR NEEDS UNIFORM. STRAY CAT pregnant. The metaphysics of small desires made newsprint, preserved until acquittal by wind. Disposed into shift.

        Back at the hostel Leila has located the missing spoon; it sits now upright inside a preserved jam jar full of cloudy water, along with a thimbleful of plumeria seeds and a folded scrap bearing a single word written in an alphabet I cannot parse. She claims the spoon and jar cured her insomnia, which had bothered her since waking inside the wrong year. I ask the story but she only smiles, initialed spoon spinning slowly within the swirl—tiny propeller—but jam jar globe will not turn the same tide twice. She gestures: tonight the seeds glow faintly, bioluminescent or simply reflecting hostel hallway LED. I settle onto my bunk beneath the slow sparkle, feeling the weight of guitar nobody plays yet nobody removes. Its silence is barricade against applause no audience remembers planning. Outside, an ambulance refrains long enough to let the ficus leaf rattle—a single elegy for continuity.

        Midafternoon, thunderhead mirroring silted river. I carry wood plank to shoreline already salt-white sandpapered. I meant to wait till dusk but the sky has pressed lower and begun flashing parentheses, as if documenting its own condition live. The water today is the colour of graphite rainfall diluted with milk, folding over shards of noonlight like reluctant origami. I kneel, gouge plank slightly into weight’s faith in gravity, set the word-list facing incoming dark. A herring gull lands ten feet away, one eye on me, other on horizon like bifocal moral. Decides I am neither predator nor breadcrumb pilgrimage sponsor, lifts off before verdict currency can be tendered.

        Here is where memory leans most precariously forward: I hear my brother’s voice preceding the thunder by exactly half-second syllable count as though they rehearsed. He always timed his jokes to thunder for maximum drama. The voice says: You still believe distances are measured only painfully? Watch plank. As soon as the plank says it, the first wave—small but certain—touches plank’s furthest edge and begins eroding all verbs simultaneously, letters curling like cellophane set briefly on hearth.

        For a blink every word fights for presences: vanish refuses vanishing, swear becomes fountain pen attempting backward walk; stay curls into anchor chain tying board itself into knot. Yet tidal eraser is stubborn. Within seven swash cycles every verb is gone. Plank blank as before speech. The herring gull has returned, stands on newly-clean slate. Voice again, fading now: distances measured otherwise ache even longer. Thunder rolls in isometric harmony.

        I remain kneeling while rain starts. Umbrella forgotten, I taste salt rain and think of plumeria seeds glowing in jar. Bright inside glass against forgetting. I lift plank—now almost weightless from layer lost—tuck keystone undercoat despite sogginess. Something must outlast moment. May vowel-less plank remember absence better than my notebooks ever managed.

        On return path streetlights still dead from power grid tantrum. Every storefront has pasted illuminated apology: OUR OUTLET TEMPORARILY BENT BY WEATHER. WE WILL REOPEN WHEN SOCKETS ALIGN. Power outages transform entire blocks into cello cases waiting instruments. I move through murk by counting windows reflecting alternate timelines: each lit room shows scenes sketched in colours none of the room’s occupants may ever guess they owned. One window reveals two men arranging flowers upright in river currents—magnetic vases intersect water flow without drifting. Another window displays woman slowly dismantling violin bridge with toothpick, midnight docent for scatter patience. Third window contains simply yellow umbrella opened upside down under exposed ceiling bulb as though practicing emergency catch for indoor lightning. None aware passerby patterns outside. I catalogue without comment, already stuffed full of mismatched atlas nerve endings.

        Hostel lobby re-stitched by generator growl. Lights intermittent like code sent by weary moths. Leila sits on threshold stoop strumming missing-string ukulele numbers. When she sees plank, she says touch word-vacant wood and nods. The absence speaks dialect we newly recognise. Ukulele fourth string replaced suddenly with fishing line retrieved from sea-smell pocket. She holds it against sounds of rain on corrugated tin roof then suddenly knots line through lowest tuning peg, miracle enough to birth entirely fresh chord neither she nor instrument knew possible. I feel keening harmonic against my teeth metal spoon once rested in jaw hollow. Somewhere upstairs generator coughs final dieseline belch, surrendering lobby back partly to candle and partly to dark.

        Night three subsequent: same summer season’s dog end foraging through alleyway bones of dumpsters. Leila and I walk toward river where unfinished bridge now carries temporary torches—welding team repairing epoxied day-after. Workers’ sparks arch mid-air like fireflies manufacturing linear constellations. Leila carries jam jar. Plumeria seeds have sprouted miniature fluorescences during interrupted power, now dance inside glass like plankton lunar repro. We choose spot beneath undecided bridge span: here river’s breadth quiet enough to hear tide reversing under bridge ribcage.

        She tips jar gently onto plank. Seeds scatter, green dots amid tide tangle. Between黑暗 river surface and torch spark above, seeds glow dual sourced: internal sugar and borrowed welding flares. Cube of reflected danger. Spoon settles rattling amidst wheatgrass larval. Leila begins humming ukulele chord re-anchored, but string whistles slightly off-tone now boat wakes lap pylons. She angles spoon so handle becomes stylus against wood bereft-of-letters. Together we wait: what new inscription does  shaken world want burnt upon this breakfast utensil?

        Spoon dances circumlit by torch flare only long enough for two lines to appear. I read them aloud once, then memory rescinds them. Cannot recall glyphs, only feeling they raised like charge through cracked powerline. After sound’s cessation, plank suddenly drenched brighter hollow where words ought to be. Plumeria seedlings root themselves where letters once resisted erosion. It is done. Bridge ahead completes its welding midnight vow, spanning dreamed gorge. Leila stops music mid-chord as cranes switch off, abrupt hush so deep it rust rings in teeth same frequency as spoon’s aftertaste.

        We leave spoon standing upright between planks like lighthouse exorcised of range. I notice on walkback constellation near eastern edge of sky has shifted to resemble outline of childhood island my brother drew: molasses shape, archipelagic tail. Either astronomy has finally conspired or this hemisphere never noticed before. I make no wishes merely observation. When I blink constellation doubles—one higher fresher sibling gently orbiting older version. Between them hangs tiny point-bright asteroid or perhaps satellite—my brother still traveling by vocabulary of distances measured otherwise.

        City returns puddle-grimed consciousness full of second-hand power. Streetlights sputter open eyed. In hostel lobby someone has stuck dried seaweed spelling the word FIN. Letters already curling at tips under fluorescent acne. Fresh mural painted on opposing wall: portrait of last week’s octopus painted entirely in bird-pecked pomegranate rind, one eye visible as daemon amber. Underneath text silhouetted: I TESTED YOU AND BECAME HUNGER. Leila plucks first whole ukulele chord since restringing: clear tonic, evenly ringing like beginning hymn.

        I discover someone has left new parcel on bunk: small matchbox wrapped in map evident to bloodroot paper, no greeting yet signature is faint smell of tidal zinc. Inside box lie seven polished pebbles—exact count of rock ritual my brother and I never finished. Pebbles cool against palm. I pocket them in place where spoons or destinations used to lodge. One pebble small enough to roll inside cheek without impediment, becoming instant worry bead / shutter dispersal when tongue worries meanings slack.

        Later—dawn still unclaimed—I rise to use communal bathroom’s one mirror undistorted by steam. Paint peels back like sour grapeskin, revealing behind plaster an photograph pasted in early hostel years: snapshot of this very bathroom rim crowded with toothbrushes clipped to hanging clothesline above tub. Every brush suspended mid-sob. A date scribbled 1997; who would have predicted its fossil inside this timeline? I peer closer noticing one toothbrush distinctive handle—recognisable as type owned by my grandmother before she gave soothsayer spoons away to impetuous sons. Ghost spatial knot completed.

        At breakfast table Leila spreads spilled sugar into continent shapes. She moves grains with skewer until archipelago forms again the island of diner napkin. I pour coffee into teal cup already holding 1.25oz invisible ocean from last night wave collision. We do not speak, because the world has ribboned temporarily to single thread of saliva-warm pebble coasting inner cheek. When Leila finally pushes sugar island gently towards my mug edge, a cafe sugar storm pelts it into reef without protests of bravery form. Storm subside into depths newly stirred. She raises eyes across table meeting mine as though catching both our reflections inside same convex tablespoon mum used to scale cocoa.

        Somewhere thunder may still rehearse lines above unfinished bridge but river below already carries plank now cleanly letter-less, now home to plumeria root. I know the spoon remains joined upriver, spoon and spoon-shaped governance. Tomorrow hostelry will empty and refill again as tides do lodgers. We who remain within rooms like shells become language when enough listened to break into voice. Outer shell memory remains the same quiet octopus yellow reserve, ileum cadmium, watery grey edging finger. If we are lucky we may taste moonlight with the nickel aftertaste of almost-loss and continue choosing thetoggle forward.

        I finish breakfast leaving pocket stone inside cheek. Chewing time gently: limestone converse. Outside, gulls practice backstroke between streetcar cables. Rind moon carves asphalt puddles ever recalibrate island boundaries drawn by our blinking. I look—not for the last time—towards horizon, and the horizon looks back with one amber eye choosing not to blink first.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
